---
format:
  html
---

## Mostly Harmless: What These Modules Are (and Aren’t) {.unnumbered}

These modules are a (hopefully) gentle introduction to the way public health uses numbers. They’re meant to be read before—or right as—courses start, whether you’re headed into Epi I, Biostat I, a research position, or you’re simply uneasy around “stats.” They are short reads which I hope you find both engaging and you can dip into at your own pace. Think of them as mini orientation, not coursework: no problem sets, no grades. You’ll see Plain-English explanations, real world examples, and visuals. And if you choose, some additional information and things to engage with to further expose you to some of this environment. Additionally I’ve themed sections to the best of my ability to make it feel more entertaining, and less “school”. Your job here is judgment and intuition—learning what a things mean, what they don’t exactly, and what to ask next.

Many people arrive reluctant to engage with numbers and a belief they simply are not good with them—not because that’s true, but because a past class or teacher made math feel hostile. The reaction based on those past experiences is understandable, but it can quietly cap what you get out of biostatistics if you let it. My own 8th grade math teacher told me I wouldn’t go anywhere because I wouldn’t record the steps of my work. An example of why in statistics, we settle for 95% confidence. The other 5% is a reminder that even the loudest opinions and the narrowest intervals can be wrong.

The point of starting now is to interrupt that cycle so you walk into week one comfortable to speak up, ask small precise questions, and give yourself permission to learn about data without feeling you’re surviving. The professors who teach Biostatistics I and II are very good at getting students where they need to go; they will do that for you too—if you put in the effort and allow them too.

The modules have a natural order: start here with Module 0 (which is most words), then move forward. There’s no stopwatch. Read in sequence, pause where something feels new, and come back as needed. The goal is comfort and clarity, not speed. Again, no grades. Success here is modest, practical, and pragmatic: a safety net that gives you steadier footing across the subject so you can follow the processes instead of white knuckling these courses. The aim is an early introduction that leaves you more at ease reading and talking about public health numbers—seeing data as observations with context (who/when/how), distinguishing ratios from rates and what each answers, understanding what distributions really are about, why odds suck (and why we still use them despite that) and expecting uncertainty to be part of any honest claim. Oh, and a module at the end talking about AI so you can grasp how they really work and what they fundamentally can’t do. It’s not about mastery; it’s about workable mental pictures, plain English language for the core ideas, and a simple habit of adding the missing context when you see a number. If that moves you from “just surviving” to moving with the material on your own terms, the modules did their job. I hope you find them helpful.
